findings.title: Intimacy, lastTriggerTimes, emotionActor are closure-scoped, no persistence
findings.description: Three pieces of critical business state live in closure variables with no persistence path. (1) `intimacy` (line 118) tracks stranger->partner progression; losing it on restart destroys user experience. (2) `lastTriggerTimes` (line 119) controls trigger cooldowns; without persistence, duplicate triggers fire immediately after restart. (3) `emotionActor` (line 117) resets to 'idle', discarding emotional continuity. CLAUDE.md explicitly forbids 'Business state in memory' and requires persistence to DB/KV/Event Store. This is the 4th consecutive review flagging this pattern (tasks 1, 5, 6, and now 7).
findings.suggestion: Persist intimacy score and lastTriggerTimes to the existing DuckDB/SQLite store. Add a persistence interface to AnimaOrchestratorDeps (e.g., `persistence?: { loadState(): Promise<PersistedState>, saveState(s: PersistedState): Promise<void> }`). Implement it against the app's existing storage in setup-orchestrator.ts. For the emotion actor, either persist state or document it as intentionally ephemeral.

findings.title: Old context-engine.ts and persona-engine.ts are now dead code
findings.description: The orchestrator replaces setupContextEngine() and setupPersonaEngine() (confirmed by the index.ts diff removing their imports and replacing with setupAnimaOrchestrator). However, the old files `context-engine.ts` (43 lines) and `persona-engine.ts` (106 lines) remain in the directory. They are no longer imported from any live entry point -- grep confirms zero imports. This is orphan code per CLAUDE.md orphan detection rules.
findings.suggestion: Delete both files: `apps/stage-tamagotchi/src/main/services/anima/context-engine.ts` and `apps/stage-tamagotchi/src/main/services/anima/persona-engine.ts`. They have been fully superseded by orchestrator.ts and setup-orchestrator.ts.

findings.title: DoNotDisturb module not wired into orchestrator
findings.description: The persona-engine exports canTrigger, recordTrigger, recordIgnore, recordUserInteraction, createDoNotDisturbState, and DoNotDisturbConfig -- a complete quiet-hours and progressive-backoff system with 222 lines of tests. None of these are imported or used in the orchestrator. handleProcessedContext (line 122) calls evaluateTriggers without first checking canTrigger(), and does not call recordTrigger() after firing. The entire DoNotDisturb system remains unreachable dead code in production despite having comprehensive unit tests. This was flagged in task-6 review and remains unaddressed.
findings.suggestion: In handleProcessedContext: (1) call canTrigger(dndState, currentHour, isFullscreen, priority, now, dndConfig) before evaluateTriggers, (2) call recordTrigger(dndState, now) after a successful trigger fire, (3) expose recordIgnore and recordUserInteraction in the AnimaOrchestrator interface, (4) add DoNotDisturb state to the orchestrator closure.

findings.title: PlaceholderVlmProvider returns hardcoded values in production DI path
findings.description: PlaceholderVlmProvider returns static hardcoded results and is instantiated unconditionally in setupAnimaOrchestrator() (line 55) with no configuration to swap in a real VLM. The pipeline runs every 60 seconds, wastes CPU on screenshot capture + pHash computation, then discards visual understanding by returning a static string. The log.warn on every call will produce ~1 warning/minute in production logs.
findings.suggestion: Either: (1) gate the screenshot pipeline behind an `enabled` config flag, (2) skip VLM calls when no real provider is configured, or (3) log the warning only once using a flag. At minimum, reduce log noise from once-per-tick to once-per-startup.

findings.title: onProactiveResponse only logs -- proactive responses never reach the UI
findings.description: The setupAnimaOrchestrator wires onProactiveResponse to call log.info only. No IPC event is emitted, no Eventa channel is used, and no contract exists in the shared directory for proactive events. When a trigger fires (e.g., rest-reminder after 2h of work), the response message is logged to the main process console but never sent to the renderer. The UI has no way to display proactive messages. This is a horizontal-only integration that does not deliver value to the user.
findings.suggestion: Define an IPC contract in `apps/stage-tamagotchi/src/shared` for proactive events. Wire onProactiveResponse to emit via Eventa or ipcMain to the renderer. The renderer should display the message in the chat/notification UI. Keep the log.info as well for observability.

findings.title: buildTriggerInput hardcodes fields, 6 of 11 triggers can never fire
findings.code_snippet:
isFirstActivityToday: false,
previousAppCategory: 'work',
matchedImportantDate: false,
hasNearDeadlineTodos: false,
windowSwitchesInLast5Min: 0,
previousFocusDurationMs: 0,
findings.description: buildTriggerInput defaults isFirstActivityToday=false, matchedImportantDate=false, hasNearDeadlineTodos=false, windowSwitchesInLast5Min=0, and previousFocusDurationMs=0. This means T01 (morning-greeting), T04 (entertainment-switch), T07 (important-date), T08 (task-due), T09 (high-frequency-switch), and T11 (return-to-desktop) can never fire. Only T03 (rest-reminder), T05 (late-night-work), and T06 (evening-summary) are potentially functional. The TRIGGER_EMOTION_MAP (line 63) defines mappings for all 11 triggers, creating a false impression of complete coverage.
findings.suggestion: Filter the triggers config to only include functional triggers (T03, T05, T06) until the missing data sources are available. Add inline comments on each hardcoded field noting which data source is needed and which task will provide it.

findings.title: No ActivityEvent source wired to the orchestrator in production
findings.description: setupAnimaOrchestrator creates and starts the orchestrator but never calls recordActivity(). No event source (Electron powerMonitor, rdev keyboard/mouse tracking, IPC from renderer) is connected. Without activity events, the ActivityMonitor sliding window is always empty, continuousWorkDurationMs is always 0, and time-based triggers can never fire. The orchestrator runs its intervals but produces no useful output because it receives no input. The test suite validates this wiring works via manual recordActivity() calls, but production has no equivalent.
findings.suggestion: Wire at least one ActivityEvent source: (1) use Electron's powerMonitor for idle detection, (2) use the existing rdev crate for keyboard/mouse activity, (3) accept activity events via IPC from the renderer, or (4) generate synthetic events from the screenshot pipeline's VLM output.

findings.title: Concurrent ticks possible for screenshot + activity intervals
findings.description: Both ScreenshotPipeline and ActivityMonitor start independent setInterval timers. While both have internal `ticking` guards (ScreenshotPipeline line 79, ActivityMonitor line 101), the orchestrator's handleProcessedContext callback mutates shared closure state (`intimacy`, `lastTriggerTimes`) from the ActivityMonitor's tick. If the screenshot pipeline's onContext callback (handleScreenshotContext) fires concurrently with an activity tick, the interleaving is safe because setScreenshotContext just sets a field. However, the handleProcessedContext function is synchronous and single-threaded in Node.js, so true concurrency is not an issue. The risk is low but the design relies on an implicit single-threaded assumption that should be documented.
findings.suggestion: Add a comment documenting the single-threaded concurrency assumption. Consider making the ticking guard explicit in the orchestrator itself rather than relying on downstream guards.

findings.title: Type assertion 'as PersonaEmotion' used without runtime guard
findings.description: Lines 138 and 194 cast emotionActor.getSnapshot().value as PersonaEmotion without a runtime check. If the xstate machine definition changes to add nested or parallel states, the snapshot value becomes an object rather than a string, and this cast silently produces incorrect values. The same pattern existed in the old persona-engine.ts and was carried over.
findings.suggestion: Create a helper function in persona-engine that safely extracts the current emotion from the actor snapshot with a runtime assertion. Export it alongside createEmotionActor to centralize the type-safe extraction.

findings.title: Test Double rationale comment on production class is misleading
findings.code_snippet:
/**
 * Electron desktopCapturer-based screenshot provider.
 * ...
 * Test Double rationale: N/A -- this is the real implementation for Electron.
 */
findings.description: Line 14 contains 'Test Double rationale: N/A -- this is the real implementation for Electron.' on the ElectronScreenshotProvider class. The 'Test Double rationale' comment pattern is reserved for test files to justify why a test double is used instead of a real dependency. Stating that a production class is NOT a test double adds noise and confuses the pattern's purpose.
findings.suggestion: Remove the 'Test Double rationale: N/A' comment from the production class. This comment pattern should only appear in test files.

positive_observations: Clean factory-function pattern (createAnimaOrchestrator) following Functional Core / Imperative Shell architecture -- pure orchestrator logic separated from Electron-specific setup
Test doubles for ScreenshotProvider and VlmProvider correctly use rationale comments and implement the real interface contracts

Comprehensive end-to-end integration test (line 372-427) exercises the full pipeline: screenshot capture -> VLM -> activity monitor -> trigger evaluation -> emotion transition -> response generation -> UI event callback
Correct use of real sharp-based pHash processing in tests (no mocking of image processing), validating actual dedup behavior
ActivityMonitor and ScreenshotPipeline both implement ticking guards to prevent concurrent execution, addressing a prior review finding
Clean DI wiring in index.ts: two separate injeca.provide calls replaced by single animaOrchestrator registration, reducing wiring complexity
Test coverage includes negative cases (under-threshold work, fullscreen suppression, intimacy-gated triggers) demonstrating thorough boundary testing
